Vision

iv 
 
PREFACE 
 
The Mathematics I Form2 Teaching Module has been developed to provide guidance to the teacher on how 
to deliver competence-based lessons which are age-appropriate for learners at this level. The teaching module 
is aimed at providing quality education that is aligned with the Competence-Based Curriculum and 21st 
Century Skills. It also aims to facilitate the building of knowledge, skills, values and positive attitudes that are 
aimed at enabling children to live and grow into productive and useful members of their communities and the 
Zambian society.  
 
The guide provided in this teaching module is aimed at facilitating the acquisition of knowledge, competences 
and values to nurture a learner who is creative, critical and an analytical thinker. The suggested learning 
activities are designed to offer learners hands-on and minds-on experiences through manipulative tasks. Thus, 
the learners will develop knowledge, desirable lifelong skills, values and positive attitudes needed for their 
personal and national development.  
 
It is hoped that the guidance provided in this module will make learning more meaningful and enjoyable as it is 
highly activity oriented and allows for a smooth transition into the Constructivist way of learning.  
 
 
 
Kelvin Mambwe (Dr) 
Permanent Secretary- Educational Services

INTRODUCTION 
The Mathematics I Form 2 Teaching Module has been developed to guide teachers on how to facilitate 
Competence-Based lessons at this level. The module aims to provide quality education that is aligned with the 
Competence-Based curriculum and 21st Century Skills, that is developing knowledge, skills, values and positive 
attitudes that are aimed at enabling learners to live and grow into productive and useful members of their 
communities and the Zambian society.  
PURPOSE OF THE MODULE 
The purpose of the module is to illustrate teaching and learning activities that: 
a) are consistent with teaching and learning in a competence-based curriculum; 
b) show processes that allow achievement of 21st Century Skills such as creativity, collaboration, 
communication and critical thinking; 
c) Illustrate what activity-based and learner-centred teaching and learning could look like. 
HOW TO USE THE MODULE 
This module provides suggested learning activities and teaching strategies that enhance learners’ 
mathematical competences. The suggested activities are designed to offer learners hands-on experiences 
through manipulation   of tasks that are interactive and learning through practice. We should bear in mind that 
the desired competences and 21st century skills will NOT be achieved by the topics ‘covered’ but by the 
manner in which teaching and learning activities are carried out. For example, asking learners to give reasons 
for their answers, whether or not the answer is correct; to plan for solving a problem; to consider alternative

ix 
 
ways of solving a problem even if the first method yields a correct answer and to consider which of the 
methods is better; to acknowledge that even in mathematics, more than one correct answer is possible. 
Activities in the Module are meant to be incorporated in the teacher’s lesson plan. The activities are NOT 
meant to replace the teacher’s lesson plan.The topics and activities in the Module are as specified in the 2024 
Mathematics Form 1to 4Syllabus. There might be terms used in the Module that are not exactly the same as 
those in the syllabus, but this is in the interest of using language that might be more learner friendly. Teachers 
are encouraged to vary the environment when conducting lessons in order to create a good atmosphere for 
learning and teaching which is learner friendly.  
Each topic in the Module begins with a key question. A key question seeks to stimulate learners’ curiosity and 
motivate them to learn. Teachers may create their own key questions that provoke their learners’ curiosity and 
motivation.

TOPIC 1:COORDINATE GEOMETRY 
INTRODUCTION  
Coordinate Geometry is a branch of Mathematics that helps us locate and describe the position of points on a 
plane using ordered pairs (𝑥, 𝑦). It connects Algebra and Geometry, allowing us to draw graphs, measure 
distances, and understand shapes more clearly. Beyond the classroom, Coordinate Geometry plays a vital role 
in everyday life. It is used in maps and Global Positioning System (GPS) to find locations, in architecture and 
engineering to design buildings and bridges, in computer graphics to create animations and video games, and 
even in navigation systems for cars and airplanes.  
Learning Coordinate Geometry equips learners with practical tools to understand both Mathematics and the 
world around them. It fosters problem-solving skills, spatial awareness, and the ability to connect abstract 
concepts with real world applications-making it an essential foundation for further studies in Mathematics, 
Science, and Technology. 
Key terms 
Geometry, cartesian plane, Origin, x-axis, y-axis, Coordinate, Ordered pairs, point, Mid- point, Gradient.

Sub-Topic 1: Cartesian Plane 
Introduction  
The Cartesian plane is a two-dimensional (2D), infinite plane formed by a horizontal x-axis and a vertical y-axis. 
These axes intersect at a central point called the origin.T he Cartesian plane helps us visualize and study 
relationships between two quantities. By plotting points, drawing lines, or curves, we can understand patterns 
and connections in both mathematics and real-life situations.

Learning activity 1: Exploring ways of describing a location (grids, maps, sitting arrangements…) 
 Activity 1.1: Location in the classroom 
Instructions:  
• 
Ensure that learners are in clearly defined rows and columns.  
• 
Ask learners to describe their own location using rows and columns. 
• 
Ask learners to find a friend’s location using rows and columns. 
Content Tips  
• Rows and columns can be numbered to make the location description easier. 
• For instance, in activity 1.1 learners can say Jane is seated on row 3 column 6

 Activity 1.2: Describing and Finding Locations in Our Environment 
Instruction: 
In small groups learners should perform the following assignment: 
Scenario 
Your school is receiving new learners. They need help finding places like the classroom, staff room, toilets, and 
playground. Your group must create a system to describe locations clearly. 
Task 1: Create a visual representation of a section of the school ( e.g  a grid map of the classroom or school or 
a simple map with landmarks …) 
Task 2: Describe Locations in 3 Different Ways 
Choose at least 3 places within the school compound (e.g., door, desk, tree, office) and describe their location 
using any or all the following: 
i. Relative position (e.g., “Next to…, behind…, in front of…”) 
ii. Directions / landmarks (e.g., “Walk straight, turn left at the office…”) 
iii. Grid (if using a grid) (e.g., “(C,4)”) 
 Task 3: Create a Challenge for Another Group 
i. 
Write 3 clues/instructions describing a hidden location

ii. Another group must follow your instructions and find it 
 Task 4: Reflection 
Answer these questions as a group: 
i. Which method of describing location was the most accurate? Why? 
ii. Which method was easiest to understand? Why? 
iii. When would you use each method in real life?

Learning activity 2: Exploring the Cartesian Plane (XOY plane) and label it (x-axis, y-axis and 
origin)  
 Activity 2.1: Cartesian plane construction 
Instructions: 
- 
Learners draw a horizontal number line. 
- 
Learners draw a vertical number line. 
- 
Learners draw both the horizontal and vertical number line by intersecting them at an appropriate 
point. Learners justify their choice of point of intersection.

16 
 
Content tips:  
• A cartesian plane is a system used to locate points on a plane.  
• In activity 2.1 learners explore how to create this system.  
• Learners may intersect the two number lines at any point of their choice. However, guidance should be 
given that the correct point of intersection of the two lines is the point where both axes are zero because 
this is the starting point of the two number lines. 
• The origin is where the x- axis and y- axis are both zero. 
• The x axis is the horizontal number line.  
• The y axis is the vertical number line. 
• The two lines divide the cartesian plane into four parts called quadrants.  
• It acts as a reference point for locating all other points on the cartesian plane. 
 
Learning activity 3: Plotting and reading ordered pairs on the XOY-plane, (x,y) starting with the 
x-coordinate followed by the y-coordinate 
     
Activity 3.1 : Human Coordinate Plane  
 
Instructions (Whole Class) 
- 
Mark out a large grid on the floor or ground 
- 
Learners to draw and label the Origin, x- axis and y- axis

17 
 
- 
One learner (leaner A) to stand on the origin while the teacher shouts out step movements (e.g. 2 
steps to the right and 3 steps up) and learner A moves to that point 
- 
Place learners in groups 
- 
Each group draws a grid and chooses two learners  
- 
Write two instructions on the board: 
• Learner A: Move 3 steps right, then 2 steps up  
• Learner B: Move 3 steps up, then 2 steps right 
- 
Each group follows the instructions and places the two learners (A and B ) on the correct spot. 
Discussion: each group to discuss the following and present to the class for consolidation 
• What did you notice? 
• Why are the final positions different?  
• What did you do differently in A and B?  
• Which movement did you do first each time? 
 
- 
After presentation learners discuss the following (after the order pair rule is discussed)  
• Points on the cartesian plane are represented using ordered pairs in which we start with the x value 
followed by y value, and is written as (𝑥, 𝑦).  
i. 
What would happen if we swapped x and y values in describing a point? 
ii. 
Why is it important for the order to remain the same on maps and graphs?

18 
 
Content tips: 
- 
Ordered Pairs are a way of representing an exact position of a point on a grid.  
- 
Each location on a plane can be described using an ordered pair, starting with x value followed by y 
value, and is written as (𝑥, 𝑦).  
- 
Switching the order gives a different location. (Eg 
o (2, 5) → 2 across, 5 up  
o (5, 2) → 5 across, 2 up 
these will give two different points

Scenario Title:The School Hall Floor Design Project 
Your school plans to renovate the floor of the assembly hall. The school wants a unique floor tile design that 
uses repeating patterns similar to those seen in floor tiles, fabric prints, or traditional textiles. The school has 
invited the mathematics club to help design these patterns using graph paper and coordinate points. 
Task:  
i. 
Plot Points: On your graph paper, plot several points using coordinates in the form (x, y). 
ii. 
Create a Shape: Join the points using straight lines to form a shape. You can create shapes such as a 
triangle, square, hexagon, or any other shape you like. 
iii. 
Repeat the Shape : Copy your shape and move it: 
• Horizontally (left or right), or 
• Vertically (up or down) 
• Do not change the size or shape, just slide it across the grid to create a repeating pattern (tessellation). 
iv. 
Form a Pattern: Keep repeating the shape until your grid has a clear pattern covering a large area. 
v. 
Add colour: colour your shapes to make the pattern neat, clear, and attractive. 
vi. 
How are repeated patterns (tessellations)used in real life? 
Note: 
• 
Keep your shape the same size when moving them. 
• 
Make sure shapes fit together without gaps or overlaps

Sub-Topic 2: Straight lines on the cartesian plane. 
Introduction 
A straight line on the cartesian plane is formed when several points are connected in straight path. Each point 
on the line has coordinates written in the form (𝑥, 𝑦). straight lines are important because they help us 
understand how two variables are related and how one quantity changes with another. In real life straight lines 
on a cartesian plane can be used to represent situations such as distance against time or Cost against number 
of items. 
Specific competence: Use straight lines to solve problems in real life 
Learning activity 1: explore the cartesian plane and its usefulness in real life (cost benefit 
analysis, demand and supply…) 
Activity1.1 : Cost Benefit Analysis on a Cartesian Plane 
Instructions 
- 
In pairs learners perform the task  
Scenario

Your community wants to start a development project but must choose the one with the best benefit for the 
cost. Using a Cartesian plane, plot the costs and benefits of each project, compare them, and decide which 
project the community should choose.

• 
x-axis → Cost (in thousands of Kwacha) 
• 
y-axis → Benefit (in thousands of Kwacha) 
•

Step 3: Draw Comparison Lines: draw straight lines from the origin (0,0) to each project point. 
Step 4: Answer the following questions 
-

i. 
has the highest benefit? 
ii. 
 has the lowest cost?

gives the best benefit compared to cost?

Content tips: 
• 
Some of the advantages of using a cartesian plane are : 
o It makes comparison faster and clearer  
o You can see relationships visually  
o It helps identify the best option quickly  
o It shows value (benefit per cost) through steepness 
- 
Steeper lines = better benefit compared to cost 
- 
Flatter lines = lower benefit compared to cost

Learning activity 3: Determining the mid-point of two points.  
Activity3.1: Middle point discovery 
Instructions:  
- 
In small groups learners should analyze the following scenario and answer the questions that follow 
 
Scenario: The constituency development committee wants to sink a bore hole at a point that is exactly halfway 
between the school and the market so that it is easy for everyone to reach. 
Use the cartesian plane to find the point that is halfway between the two locations and state its coordinates, 
given that the school is at (2,4) and the market is at (8,6).

39 
 
Note:  
If the learners are not able to generalize the a way of determining the midpoint the teacher can ask the 
following guiding questions to prompt the derivation. 
i. 
Compare the x coordinate of the two given points with the x coordinate of the half way point. 
ii. 
Compare the y coordinate of the two given points with the y coordinate of the half way point. 
iii. 
Using your observations suggest the formula for calculating the half way point 
Content tips:

TOPIC 2: RELATIONS AND FUNCTIONS 
INTRODUCTION  
Relations and functions are important concepts in Mathematics that help us understand how different quantities are 
connected. A relation describes a relationship between two sets of values, showing how elements from one set 
correspond to elements in another. A function is a special type of relation where each input is linked to exactly one 
output. Relations and functions are widely used in areas such as science, economics, engineering, and technology, 
helping us understand how one quantity changes in response to another. 
Key terms: Relation, Function, Domain, Range, Ordered Pair, Mapping (Arrow Diagram), Input, Output, 
Independent Variable, Dependent Variable, One-to-One Relation, Many-to-One Relation, One-to-Many Relation, Many-
to-Many Relation 
General competence(s): 
Analytical Thinking, Problem Solving, Creativity, Critical Thinking, Collaboration, Financial Literacy, Digital 
Literacy 
 
 
 
In everyday life, some quantities depend 
on 
others, 
such 
as 
money 
earned 
depending 
on 
hours 
worked. 
How can mathematics help us represent 
and understand these relationships?

70 
 
Sub-Topic 1: Relations 
Introduction  
Relations help us describe and understand connections between different quantities or sets. In Mathematics, 
relations can be represented in various ways such as ordered pairs, tables, arrow diagrams (mappings), and 
graphs. These representations allow us to analyse how elements in one set are linked to elements in another set 
and help us determine different types of relations such as one-to-one, many-to-one, one-to-many, and many-
to-many. 
Specific competence: Apply relations to model and solve problems

• Groups present their solutions and justifications to the entire class.  
• After the presentations, in their groups, learners to assign the conventional names (one to one, one to 
many, many to one, many to many) to each of the relations described. 
Content tips: 
• The connection between the elements of the two sets is known as the relation between them. 
• A relation describes how elements in one set are connected or matched to elements in another set. 
• When each item in the first set is connected to only one item in the second set, and each item in the 
second set is also connected to only one item in the first set, the pair shows a one-to-one relationship. 
• When one item in the first set is connected to more than one item in the second set, while each item in 
the second set is connected to only one item in the first set, the pair shows a one-to-many relationship. 
• When several items in the first set are connected to the same item in the second set, the pair shows a 
many-to-one relationship. 
• When several items in the first set are connected to several items in the second set, the pair shows a 
many-to-many relationship.

Sub-Topic 2: Functions 
Introduction 
Functions are a special type of relation that describe a clear connection between two quantities. In a relation, 
elements can be connected in different ways, but in a function each element in the first set is connected to 
only one element in the second set. Studying functions helps us understand how one quantity depends on 
another, identify patterns, make predictions, and solve real-life problems. 
 
Specific competence: Apply Functions to model and solve problems

TOPIC 4: POLYGONS 
Introduction: 
 
Polygons surround us in ways both obvious and subtle. They are two-dimensional shapes that form surfaces of 
three-dimensional shapes such as pavers, road signs, windows and in the intricate geometric patterns woven 
into traditional fabrics and crafts passed down through generations. These shapes formed by straight lines 
meeting at angles are more than mere drawings on paper; they represent order, symmetry, and structure in the 
world around us

 Regular Polygons have:

  Activity 5.2 : Project- ‘Inter concept connection’ 
Instructions: 
- 
In groups learners perform the following project  
- 
Learners should be given enough time to complete the project  
- 
Do not limit the task to the materials listed, use locally available materials and adapt the project to 
school/ community specifications.  
 Projects outline 
“You are a team of landscape designers. Your task is to design and build a small school garden using different 
polygon-shaped sections for planting.” 
 Materials: String or rope, Pegs/sticks, Measuring tape, Protractor, Chalk or lime powder (for marking ground), 
Graph paper (for planning).  
Project  Procedure  
Part 1: Planning the Garden (On Paper) 
i. 
Draw a coordinate grid with appropriate scale, using the first quadrant only  (e.g., 1 unit = 1 meter). 
ii. 
Design your garden layout including: 
o At least 4 garden beds (polygons): 
▪ 
One regular polygon (e.g., square, hexagon)

169 
 
▪ 
One triangle 
▪ 
One irregular (non-regular) polygon 
▪ 
One polygon with more than 4 sides 
iii. 
Label all vertices with coordinates. 
iv. 
Calculate perimeter to estimate fencing needed 
v. 
Calculate area to estimate soil required 
Part 2: Transferring to the Ground 
i. 
Choose an open space at school. 
ii. 
Mark an origin point (0,0). 
iii. 
Use measuring tape to locate and mark coordinates using pegs. 
iv. 
Join the pegs using rope to form each polygon. 
v. 
Calculate actual perimeter built 
vi. 
Calculate actual area  
Part 3: Angle Investigation 
For each polygon: 
i. 
Calculate the expected angle sum

170 
 
ii. 
Measure actual angles using a protractor (or estimate using constructed corners) 
iii. 
Record all results in a table 
Part 4: Error Calculation 
For each polygon, calculate: 
i. 
Absolute error of the perimeter 
ii. 
Percentage error of the perimeter 
iii. 
Percentage error of the angle sum  
Part 5: Garden Reflection and Improvement  
Learners discuss and write: 
i. Which shape was easiest to construct on the ground? Why? 
ii. Which polygon had the largest error? 
iii. What caused the errors? (e.g., rope movement, uneven ground, inaccurate measuring) 
iv. How can the garden layout be improved? 
v. Why is accuracy important in real-life construction? 
vi. If you have the same amount of land, which shape of garden bed would give you the most efficient use 
of space and why?

171

Adapt the project to suit locally available materials.  
- 
Learners will apply knowledge from several concepts.
